primary_diagnosis.name: Partial-thickness thermal flame burn of the anterior trunk and face (~15% TBSA) with suspected thermal inhalation/airway injury

pathophysiology_summary: A gas-barbeque flash fire delivered a brief, high-intensity thermal load to exposed skin and the upper airway. The skin injury is partial-thickness (erythema, blistering, epidermal sloughing) over roughly 15% of the total body surface area of the anterior trunk and face; capillary leak produces local oedema and, over hours, a systemic fluid shift risking hypovolaemic (burn) shock — hence the need for formal fluid resuscitation (e.g. Parkland-type). The parallel and more immediately life-threatening problem is thermal injury to the upper airway: hot gas causes progressive mucosal oedema of the supraglottis. The tetrad of facial burns, singed nasal hair/eyebrows, carbonaceous sputum and hoarse voice strongly predicts an inhalation injury. Airway oedema can evolve from a patient who is currently speaking in full sentences to complete obstruction within minutes to hours, which is why early (prophylactic) intubation before the swelling worsens is standard. Carbon-monoxide/cyanide exposure from combustion can also depress oxygen delivery independent of a near-normal SpO2 reading.

differential.diagnosis: thermal flame burn with possible inhalation injury
differential.evidence_basis: Acute burning pain and immediate blistering of face and chest after a gas-flame flash fire; hoarse voice raises airway concern.

differential.diagnosis: isolated cutaneous burn without airway involvement
differential.evidence_basis: Possible if there were no facial/airway features — but facial burn, singed hair and hoarse voice argue against isolation to skin.
Considered with any burn, but the clear flame/flash-fire mechanism and absence of a corrosive agent make it unlikely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, a flame burn to the face with voice change should immediately raise inhalation injury and airway risk — not be treated as a simple skin burn.

differential.diagnosis: partial-thickness thermal burn with thermal inhalation/airway injury
differential.evidence_basis: Facial burn + singed nasal hair + carbonaceous sputum + hoarse voice + throat tightness = classic inhalation-injury constellation with impending airway compromise.
History is a gas flash fire, not exposure to a corrosive liquid or powder.
Immediate blistering, singed facial hair and an acute flame mechanism exclude simple sunburn.
Hoarseness and throat tightness began within minutes of a hot-gas blast — thermal airway oedema, not a viral aetiology.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The differentials in the source (chemical burn, sunburn, URTI) are all confidently excluded once the flame mechanism and airway features are elicited; the remaining task is triage and escalation, not further diagnostic refinement.

differential.diagnosis: partial-thickness thermal flame burn (~15% TBSA) with suspected thermal inhalation/airway injury
differential.evidence_basis: Time-critical: airway oedema may progress to obstruction, and a ~15% TBSA partial-thickness burn needs formal fluid resuscitation and specialist burn-centre care.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive severity (exact %TBSA, depth, carboxyhaemoglobin, electrolytes, degree of airway narrowing) requires in-person assessment and hospital investigations — telehealth can only presume and escalate.

red_flags.description: Facial burn with singed nasal hair/eyebrows, carbonaceous sputum and hoarse voice — inhalation-injury constellation with impending upper-airway obstruction
red_flags.clinical_significance: Predicts thermal airway oedema; the airway is the immediate threat to life and can occlude within minutes to hours.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed escalation risks a lost airway at home — an unsurvivable airway disaster without prophylactic intubation.

red_flags.description: Throat tightness with hoarse voice, currently still speaking in full sentences (early/soft airway signs)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Soft signs precede stridor; the ability to speak now is falsely reassuring — the window to secure the airway is before frank obstruction.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Waiting for stridor or hypoxia before acting can leave too little time to secure a swollen, distorted airway.

red_flags.description: Major partial-thickness burn ~15% TBSA — risk of burn (hypovolaemic) shock from fluid shift
red_flags.clinical_significance: A burn of this size requires formal fluid resuscitation; untreated fluid loss into the burn causes hypovolaemic shock.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-resuscitation leads to shock, renal injury and increased burn depth.

red_flags.description: Possible carbon monoxide / combustion-gas toxicity despite near-normal pulse oximetry
red_flags.clinical_significance: SpO2 can read normal in CO poisoning because carboxyhaemoglobin is misread as oxyhaemoglobin; true oxygenation may be impaired.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: False reassurance from a normal home oximeter reading can mask serious hypoxia.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Focusing on the dramatic blistered chest and treating this as a simple skin burn, missing the airway threat signalled by the facial burn, singed hair, sooty sputum and hoarse voice.
Being reassured that the patient can still speak in full sentences and that SpO2 is ~95% — thermal airway oedema is progressive and the safe time to act is before obstruction.
Accepting a near-normal SpO2 as evidence of adequate oxygenation; standard pulse oximetry cannot distinguish carboxyhaemoglobin, so it can be falsely reassuring after smoke/combustion exposure.
Attempting to manage a major facial/inhalation burn with home first aid and a GP follow-up instead of recognising a time-critical emergency and calling 000 for a burn centre.

risk_scores.tool_name: Estimated %TBSA (Rule of Nines / Lund-Browder — clinician estimate)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: approximately 15% TBSA partial-thickness, anterior trunk and face
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Exceeds the threshold for formal fluid resuscitation and referral to a specialist burn service; combined with airway features it mandates emergency transport.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Accurate %TBSA and burn depth (partial vs full thickness)
Exact blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate and reliable SpO2
Auscultated air entry / degree of expiratory wheeze
Serum electrolytes (hyponatraemia / hyperkalaemia from fluid shifts)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 for a Priority 1 ambulance to a specialised burn centre; keep the patient upright and calm, nil by mouth, continue/repeat cool running water to the burn and cover loosely with a clean non-adherent layer or cling film, and monitor breathing continuously for any harsh/noisy breathing (stridor) or drop in responsiveness.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the burn diagnosis (mechanism and features are classic); the real risk is UNDER-action — under-triaging the airway because the patient can currently speak, or managing at home rather than escalating for airway protection and fluid resuscitation.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ANZBA Emergency Management of Severe Burns (EMSB)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Assess for inhalation injury (facial burns, singed nasal hair, carbonaceous sputum, hoarse voice); consider early airway protection; commence formal fluid resuscitation for burns exceeding the size threshold; refer to a specialist burn service.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ANZCOR Guideline 9.1.3 — First aid management of a burn
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Cool the burn with cool running water for 20 minutes (effective up to 3 hours after injury), remove non-adherent clothing/jewellery, cover loosely with a clean non-stick dressing or cling film, keep the patient warm, and seek urgent medical care for large or facial/airway burns.
